text: The surgeon performs dissection from medial to lateral above the sacral promontory, carefully separating tissue layers in the pelvic region.
The surgeon bridges the peritoneum and continues the dissection anteriorly, focusing on the visible tissue layers.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery from its attachments, clearing the surrounding tissue. Three hemolog clips are applied: two on the proximal side and one on the distal side, which is the specimen side.
The anatomical structure is detached following the dissection process, completing the separation from its attachments.
The surgeon continues the dissection from medial to lateral, detaching Girota's fascia from the colon.
The surgeon continues the dissection towards the DJ flexure, carefully identifying and dissecting the inferior mesenteric vein near this anatomical landmark.
The surgeon applies hemlock clips to the inferior mesenteric artery, with two clips placed on the proximal side and one on the distal side, similar to the previous clipping technique.
The surgeon carefully navigates near the tail of the pancreas after dissecting the inferior mesenteric vein, with the option to enter the lesser sac visible in the surgical field.
A swab is placed in the surgical field, likely to absorb fluids or maintain a clear view of the operative area.
The surgeon continues the dissection from the lateral to medial side, joining the previously dissected medial to lateral plane, effectively connecting both dissection paths.
The surgeon detaches the gastrocolic ligament from the colon, providing access to the lesser sac and facilitating the complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon continues dissection into the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly, advancing the surgical field within the colorectal region.
A swab is strategically placed to provide retraction while maintaining the integrity of the TME plane during the dissection.
The surgeon continues dissection posteriorly and laterally, carefully avoiding nerve damage, and proceeds with anterior dissection within the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates the narrow pelvis, detaching the specimen laterally while maintaining careful dissection within the TME plane.
The surgeon completes the dissection by joining the dissected tissue planes from all sides, ensuring the integrity of the TME package.
The surgeon approaches the distal margin at the pelvic floor, taking care to avoid damaging the prostate and seminal vesicles during anterior dissection.
The surgeon identifies the fascia of Denonvilliers while using suction to clear smoke and maintain a clear view within the narrow pelvic cavity.
The surgical field shows minimal blood loss as the dissection progresses within the narrow pelvis, with careful manipulation to avoid damage to surrounding structures.
The surgeon prepares the area for transection at the level of the pelvis, performing a sigmoidoscopy to determine the precise level for transection.
The surgeon uses a 45mm Endo GIA stapler twice to transect the prepared area at the level of the pelvis, ensuring precise tissue separation.

The surgeon retrieves a large specimen from the pelvis, revealing a clear and clean pelvic cavity. Both nerves and the presacral fascia remain intact, with the staple line and seminal vesicles clearly visible and undamaged. There is no blood loss observed.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end coloanal anastomosis using a circular stapler. Subsequently, a proximal ileostomy is created.
